flashcards.front: What is the #include preprocessor directive in C++?
flashcards.back: #include is a preprocessor directive that tells the compiler to include the contents of a header file before compiling. For example, #include <iostream> includes input/output functionality.

flashcards.front: What is the main() function in C++?
flashcards.back: The main() function is the entry point of every C++ program. Execution of the program begins and ends with this function.
int main() returns an integer value to the operating system. Returning 0 indicates successful program execution, while non-zero values indicate errors.

flashcards.front: What are the two types of comments in C++?
flashcards.back: Single-line comments (//) - comment everything after it on that line; Multi-line comments (/* ... */) - comment everything between the delimiters.

flashcards.front: What is 'using namespace std;' in C++?
flashcards.back: It tells the compiler to use the standard (std) namespace, allowing use of standard library elements like cout and cin without the std:: prefix.
cin is the standard input stream object used to read data from the keyboard (standard input). It is used with the extraction operator (>>).
cout is the standard output stream object used to display output to the screen (standard output). It is used with the insertion operator (<<).
endl is a manipulator that inserts a newline character and flushes the output buffer. It is equivalent to '\n' but also forces the output to display immediately.

flashcards.front: What is a header file in C++?
flashcards.back: A header file is a file containing declarations and definitions (like function prototypes, classes, macros) that can be shared across multiple source files.
return 0; at the end of main() indicates that the program executed successfully without errors. It returns control to the operating system.

flashcards.front: What is the basic structure of a C++ program?
flashcards.back: The basic structure includes: Preprocessor directives (#include), using namespace (optional), int main() declaration, opening brace {, statements, return 0;, and closing brace }.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between // and /* */ comments?
flashcards.back: // (single-line) comments everything until the end of that line only. /* */ (multi-line) comments everything between the opening and closing symbols, spanning multiple lines.

flashcards.front: What is the iostream header file?
flashcards.back: iostream provides input/output functionality in C++. It defines objects like cin for input and cout for output, along with operators << and >>.

flashcards.front: What is a namespace in C++?
flashcards.back: A namespace is a declarative region that groups related identifiers (functions, classes, variables) to avoid naming conflicts. The std namespace contains standard library elements.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between endl and '\n'?
flashcards.back: endl inserts a newline AND flushes the output buffer, ensuring immediate display. '\n' only inserts a newline without flushing, which is slightly more efficient.
